core_thesis: @didoriot applies marketing and cultural critique to internet trends, influencer aesthetics, and consumer behavior — treating brand decisions, platform dynamics, and online identity as legitimate objects of analysis. The creator operates from inside the culture they critique: a salaried professional who also writes on Substack, crosses between TikTok and Instagram, and is openly figuring out the creator economy in real time. The throughline is the tension between aspiration and reality — in careers, in consumption, in how people perform themselves online.
key_ideas: Brand marketing critique — reading cultural moments (Coachella, Goop, Starbucks) as strategic brand moves
Aspirational creator economy — the Substack writer and influencer as career archetypes, including their mystified income
AI as cultural object — not just a tool but a discourse to be analyzed and pushed back against
Parasocial performance and the 'online boyfriend' — how parasocial dynamics are consciously managed by both creators and audiences
Overconsumption / underconsumption aesthetics — frugal chic and the aestheticization of restraint as a market trend
Gen Alpha anxiety — worrying about what platform-native childhoods produce in taste, values, and identity
Corporate life as content source — the office as a site of irony, observation, and material (elevator behavior, getting to work early)
Personal sobriety and lifestyle as quiet counternarrative — opting out of default social scripts
